claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 10 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let me ask you a few questions to assess your eligibility.

**First, I need to know about your child's age:**

Is your child:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **Between 16-19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know which age category your child falls into.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - Alex is 10 years old, so under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information! Alex is 10 years old, so under 16. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about Alex:**

Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?

(Since Alex is only 10 years old, I expect the answer is yes - most 10-year-olds are not in paid work - but I need to confirm this to continue.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, Alex doesn't work at all - he's just a kid!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That makes sense. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

Is Alex **NOT** receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

(Again, since Alex is 10 years old, I expect they're not receiving these benefits, but I need to confirm this.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't get any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship program?

(Since Alex is 10 years old, I expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm. Apprenticeships typically start at age 16 or older.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in an apprenticeship - he's only 10!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely right! Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now I need to ask about your residency status:**

Do you meet the UK residency requirements? You qualify if **ANY** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (for up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support your family
6. You have pre-settled status as a student with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

Do any of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirements. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about your responsibility for Alex:**

Are you responsible for Alex? You qualify if **ANY** of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex**
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care - e.g., food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents)
3. You pay maintenance for Alex under a court order or agreement

Do any of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex doesn't live with me at the moment. I do help out a bit with things like clothes and school dinners - I contribute about £20 a week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Let me check what the current Child Benefit rate is to see if your £20 weekly contribution meets the requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I can see that the exact current Child Benefit rates aren't specified in my reference materials, but I can tell you that the current rates (as of 2024) are approximately:
- **£25.60 per week** for the eldest or only child
- **£16.95 per week** for additional children

You mentioned you contribute about **£20 per week** towards Alex's care (clothes and school dinners). This is less than the standard Child Benefit rate of approximately £25.60 per week for an only child.

**Important question:** 
- Is Alex your eldest or only child that you're claiming for?
- Are your contributions the full extent of your financial support, or is there anything else you provide (like housing costs, other regular expenses, presents, pocket money, etc.)?

The requirement is that you need to contribute **at least** the equivalent of what Child Benefit would pay towards Alex's upkeep.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is my only child. The £20 a week is what I'm able to contribute at the moment - that's it really, just helping with clothes and school dinners when I can.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for being honest about your situation. I need to be clear with you about this:

To qualify for Child Benefit based on contributing to a child's upkeep (when you don't live with them), you need to contribute **at least the same amount as Child Benefit would pay** - which is approximately **£25.60 per week** for an only child.

Since you're contributing **£20 per week**, this falls short of the requirement by about £5.60 per week.

Based on the eligibility rules, this means you would not meet the "responsibility for the child" requirement at this current contribution level.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Final Assessment Result: **NOT ELIGIBLE**

I'm sorry, but based on the Child Benefit eligibility rules, **you are not currently eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

### Here's why:

**What's in your favor:**
- ✓ Alex is a qualifying child (10 years old, under 16, not working, not receiving benefits)
- ✓ You meet the UK residency requirements (you live in Manchester)

**The issue:**
- ✗ You don't meet the "responsibility for the child" requirement

### The Responsibility Requirement Explained:

To be eligible for Child Benefit, you must be responsible for the child, which means **either**:
1. **Living with the child**, OR
2. **Contributing at least the Child Benefit amount** (approximately £25.60 per week for an only child) towards their care

Since Alex doesn't live with you and your current contribution is £20 per week, this falls short of the required £25.60 per week threshold.

### What You Could Consider:

- **If you could increase your contribution** to at least £25.60 per week (through a combination of money, clothes, food, school expenses, presents, etc.), you would meet the responsibility requirement
- **The person Alex lives with** may be eligible to claim Child Benefit if they meet the other requirements
- If you have a **court-ordered maintenance agreement**, this could also count towards the responsibility requirement

I know this isn't the answer you were hoping for, but I hope this explanation helps you understand the rules. If your circumstances change, you may become eligible in the future.

reasonings: Alex is 10 years old (under 16), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship - so Alex is a qualifying child. The claimant meets UK residency requirements as they live in Manchester. However, the claimant does not meet the 'responsibility for the child' requirement. Alex does not live with the claimant, and the claimant's financial contribution of £20 per week falls short of the required amount (approximately £25.60 per week for an only child). To qualify based on financial contribution alone, the claimant must contribute at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit payment towards Alex's upkeep.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex because they do not meet the responsibility requirement. While Alex qualifies as an eligible child and the claimant meets residency requirements, the claimant's weekly contribution of £20 is below the threshold of approximately £25.60 per week required when not living with the child.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.guidance: You must be responsible for the child, which means either living with them OR paying at least the Child Benefit amount towards their care (food, clothes, housing, pocket money, etc.). This can include maintenance payments under court order.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 10 years old (under 16), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship - so Alex is a qualifying child. The claimant meets UK residency requirements as they live in Manchester. However, the claimant does not meet the 'responsibility for the child' requirement. Alex does not live with the claimant, and the claimant's financial contribution of £20 per week falls short of the required amount (approximately £25.60 per week for an only child). To qualify based on financial contribution alone, the claimant must contribute at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit payment towards Alex's upkeep.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex because they do not meet the responsibility requirement. While Alex qualifies as an eligible child and the claimant meets residency requirements, the claimant's weekly contribution of £20 is below the threshold of approximately £25.60 per week required when not living with the child.
